title: Classic Spaghetti Carbonara
intro: Silky ribbons of spaghetti coated in a glossy egg and cheese sauce studded with crisp guanciale. This Roman classic relies on heat and timing rather than cream for its luxurious finish.
ingredients: 150 g guanciale, cut into small strips
Fine salt for the pasta water
steps: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a rolling boil and cook the spaghetti until just al dente.
While the pasta cooks, fry the guanciale in a cold dry pan over medium heat until the fat renders and the strips turn golden and crisp.
Whisk the egg yolks, whole egg, both cheeses and the black pepper together in a bowl until smooth.
Reserve a mugful of starchy pasta water, then drain the spaghetti and add it to the pan with the guanciale, off the heat.
Pour in the egg mixture and toss vigorously, adding splashes of the reserved water until a creamy sauce forms.
Serve immediately in warm bowls with an extra dusting of Pecorino and more cracked pepper.
tip: Always add the eggs off the heat so they thicken into a sauce instead of scrambling.

title: Tagliatelle al Ragu Bolognese
intro: A deeply savoury meat sauce simmered slowly with milk and wine until rich and velvety. Traditionally cloaked over fresh tagliatelle rather than spaghetti for the perfect cling.
ingredients: Salt, pepper and grated Parmesan to serve
steps: Warm the olive oil in a heavy pot and gently soften the onion, carrot, celery and pancetta until fragrant.
Raise the heat, add the beef and pork, and brown thoroughly while breaking up any clumps.
Pour in the milk and simmer until it is almost fully absorbed, then repeat with the white wine.
Stir in the chopped tomatoes and tomato paste, season, and bring to a gentle bubble.
Lower the heat and simmer uncovered for at least two and a half hours, stirring occasionally until glossy and thick.
Cook the tagliatelle in salted boiling water until al dente, then toss with a few ladles of the ragu.
Plate up and finish each serving with plenty of grated Parmesan.
tip: The milk added early tenderises the meat and rounds out the sauce, so do not skip it.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
intro: A humble Neapolitan midnight favourite built from little more than garlic, good olive oil and chilli. Simple, fast and endlessly satisfying when you crave big flavour with few ingredients.
ingredients: Small handful of flat-leaf parsley, chopped
steps: Cook the spaghetti in a large pot of salted boiling water until al dente.
Meanwhile, warm the olive oil in a wide pan and add the sliced garlic over gentle heat.
Cook the garlic slowly until pale gold and fragrant, then stir in the chilli flakes.
Reserve a cup of pasta water, drain the spaghetti, and add it to the garlicky oil.
Toss well with a splash of the reserved water until the pasta glistens and emulsifies.
Fold through the parsley, adjust the salt, and serve at once with Parmesan if you like.
tip: Keep the heat low while cooking the garlic, as burnt garlic turns the whole dish bitter.

title: Penne alla Vodka
intro: A blushing tomato and cream sauce brightened by a splash of vodka that coaxes out deep, rounded flavour. Comforting yet a little elegant, it clings beautifully to ridged penne.
ingredients: Salt, pepper and fresh basil to serve
steps: Heat the olive oil in a large pan and soften the onion and garlic with the chilli flakes.
Pour in the vodka and let it bubble for a minute or two to cook off the raw edge.
Add the crushed tomatoes, season, and simmer gently for around fifteen minutes until thickened.
Stir in the cream and warm through until the sauce turns a rich blush colour.
Cook the penne in salted boiling water until al dente, reserving a little pasta water.
Toss the drained pasta into the sauce with the Parmesan, loosening with pasta water as needed.
Finish with torn basil and an extra grating of cheese.
tip: Let the vodka simmer long enough to mellow, or the sauce can taste sharp.

title: Linguine alle Vongole
intro: Fresh clams steamed open in white wine and garlic, their briny liquor becoming the sauce itself. A coastal Italian classic that tastes of the sea with every forkful.
ingredients: 1 kg fresh clams, cleaned and purged
steps: Begin cooking the linguine in salted boiling water until just short of al dente.
Warm the olive oil in a large deep pan and gently cook the garlic and chilli flakes until fragrant.
Add the clams and the white wine, then cover and steam until the shells open, discarding any that stay shut.
Reserve a little pasta water, then lift the drained linguine straight into the pan with the clams.
Toss over the heat for a minute so the pasta drinks up the briny sauce, loosening with pasta water if needed.
Scatter over the parsley and serve immediately in warm bowls.
tip: Soak the clams in salted water beforehand so they release any grit before cooking.

title: Cacio e Pepe
intro: Just cheese, pepper and pasta transformed into a creamy Roman masterpiece through technique alone. The starchy water and Pecorino emulsify into a clinging, peppery sauce.
ingredients: 2 tsp whole black peppercorns, freshly ground
steps: Cook the pasta in a modest amount of well-salted boiling water so the starch stays concentrated.
Toast the freshly ground black pepper in a dry pan for a moment to release its aroma.
Ladle a little hot pasta water into the pan with the pepper and let it bubble gently.
In a bowl, mix the grated Pecorino with a splash of cooler pasta water to form a smooth paste.
Drain the pasta just before al dente and toss it in the peppery pan with more pasta water.
Remove from the heat, stir in the cheese paste, and toss until a glossy sauce coats every strand.
tip: Let the pasta water cool slightly before mixing with the cheese to avoid clumping.

title: Pappardelle with Slow-Braised Beef Ragu
intro: Tender beef shin braised in red wine until it falls into rich, silky shreds. Draped over broad pappardelle, it is the ultimate cold-weather comfort dish.
ingredients: 1 kg beef shin, cut into chunks
Salt, pepper and Parmesan to serve
steps: Season the beef generously and brown it in batches in a hot casserole with olive oil, then set aside.
Lower the heat and soften the onions, carrots, celery and garlic in the same pot.
Pour in the red wine and scrape up the browned bits, letting it reduce by half.
Return the beef with the tomatoes, stock and rosemary, then bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover and braise in a low oven for around three hours until the meat shreds easily.
Shred the beef into the sauce and adjust the seasoning until deeply savoury.
Cook the pappardelle until al dente, toss with the ragu, and finish with grated Parmesan.
tip: Brown the beef properly in batches, as crowding the pan steams the meat and dulls the flavour.

title: Pasta alla Norma
intro: A Sicilian celebration of golden fried aubergine, sweet tomato sauce and salty ricotta salata. Rustic, vibrant and named in honour of a beloved opera.
ingredients: 5 tbsp olive oil, plus extra for frying
steps: Salt the cubed aubergine and let it sit for fifteen minutes, then pat dry to draw out moisture.
Fry the aubergine in hot olive oil until golden on all sides, then drain on paper towels.
In a clean pan, gently cook the garlic in olive oil, then add the tomatoes and simmer until thickened.
Season the sauce, tear in most of the basil, and stir the fried aubergine back through.
Cook the rigatoni in salted boiling water until al dente and reserve a little pasta water.
Toss the pasta with the sauce, loosening with pasta water so it coats every piece.
Serve topped with grated ricotta salata and the remaining basil.
tip: Salting the aubergine first keeps it from soaking up too much oil while frying.

title: Fettuccine Alfredo
intro: Ribbons of fettuccine bathed in a glossy emulsion of butter and Parmesan. The original Roman version uses no cream, relying on cheese and starchy water for its richness.
steps: Cook the fettuccine in salted boiling water until just al dente, reserving plenty of pasta water.
Melt half the butter with a ladle of hot pasta water in a wide pan to form a milky base.
Add the drained pasta and the remaining butter, tossing until the butter melts and coats the strands.
Remove the pan from the heat and gradually shower in the grated Parmesan while tossing constantly.
Loosen with more pasta water as needed until a smooth, glossy sauce forms.
Season with black pepper and serve immediately while hot and creamy.
tip: Work off the heat when adding the cheese so it melts smoothly instead of turning stringy.

title: Orecchiette with Broccoli Rabe and Sausage
intro: Ear-shaped pasta from Puglia tossed with bitter greens, garlic and crumbled sausage. A rustic southern dish balancing savoury, spicy and pleasantly bitter notes.
steps: Blanch the broccoli rabe in salted boiling water for two minutes, then lift it out and roughly chop.
Cook the orecchiette in the same pot of boiling water until al dente.
Meanwhile, brown the crumbled sausage in olive oil until golden, breaking it into small pieces.
Add the garlic and chilli flakes, cooking until fragrant, then stir in the chopped broccoli rabe.
Reserve some pasta water, drain the orecchiette, and add it to the pan with the sausage and greens.
Toss everything together with a splash of pasta water, then finish with grated Pecorino.
tip: Blanching the broccoli rabe first tames its bitterness while keeping its bright colour.

title: Spaghetti alla Puttanesca
intro: A boldly flavoured Neapolitan sauce of tomatoes, olives, capers and anchovies. Punchy, salty and aromatic, it comes together quickly from pantry staples.
ingredients: 100 g black olives, pitted and halved
steps: Warm the olive oil in a pan and melt the anchovies with the garlic and chilli flakes until dissolved.
Stir in the olives and capers, letting them sizzle briefly to release their aroma.
Add the chopped tomatoes and simmer gently for around fifteen minutes until the sauce thickens.
Meanwhile, cook the spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente.
Reserve a little pasta water, drain the spaghetti, and toss it through the sauce.
Loosen with pasta water if needed, scatter over the parsley, and serve at once.
tip: The anchovies, olives and capers are salty, so taste before adding any extra salt.

title: Trofie al Pesto Genovese
intro: Hand-rolled Ligurian trofie tossed with a fragrant basil pesto, potatoes and green beans. The vibrant sauce is pounded fresh for the truest, brightest flavour.
steps: Pound the basil, pine nuts, garlic and a pinch of salt into a paste with a mortar and pestle.
Work in both cheeses, then slowly stream in the olive oil until a loose green sauce forms.
Cook the diced potato in salted boiling water for a few minutes, then add the trofie and green beans.
Cook everything together until the pasta is al dente and the vegetables are tender.
Reserve a little pasta water, then drain the pasta, potato and beans together.
Toss with the pesto off the heat, loosening with pasta water until silky, and serve right away.
tip: Never heat the pesto directly, as high heat dulls its colour and fresh basil aroma.

title: Baked Ziti with Mozzarella
intro: Tubes of ziti folded into a hearty tomato sauce, layered with cheese and baked until bubbling. A crowd-pleasing bake with a golden, melty top and a soft, saucy centre.
steps: Soften the onion and garlic in olive oil, then add the passata and simmer until thickened and seasoned.
Cook the ziti in salted boiling water until just shy of al dente, then drain.
Toss the pasta with most of the tomato sauce, the ricotta and the torn basil until well coated.
Spoon half the mixture into a baking dish and scatter over half the mozzarella.
Layer in the remaining pasta, then top with the rest of the sauce, mozzarella and Parmesan.
Bake in a hot oven for around twenty-five minutes until golden and bubbling.
Let it rest for a few minutes before serving so the layers hold together.
tip: Undercook the pasta slightly since it continues softening in the oven as it bakes.

title: Roast Chicken with Fresh Thyme
intro: A gloriously simple roast that relies on high heat and a bone-dry skin to deliver shattering crackle over juicy meat. Nothing but salt, pepper, and heat between you and perfection.
steps: Pat the chicken completely dry inside and out, then let it sit uncovered in the fridge for an hour if time allows.
Heat the oven to 450F and season the cavity and skin generously with salt and pepper.
Truss the legs together with kitchen string and tuck the wing tips underneath the body.
Set the bird breast-up in a roasting pan or ovenproof skillet and slide it into the center of the oven.
Roast undisturbed for about 50 to 60 minutes until the skin is deep golden and the thigh juices run clear.
Rest the chicken for 15 minutes, then brush the skin with softened butter whisked with mustard and thyme leaves.
Carve and serve, spooning the pan juices over each portion.
tip: Do not baste or open the oven; a stable high heat gives you the crispest skin.

title: Classic Coq au Vin
intro: Chicken braised slowly in red wine with smoky bacon, mushrooms, and pearl onions until the sauce turns velvety and deep. This is French country cooking at its most soul-warming.
steps: Brown the bacon in a Dutch oven, then remove it and sear the seasoned chicken in the fat until golden on all sides.
Set the chicken aside and soften the pearl onions and garlic in the same pot.
Sprinkle in the flour and stir for a minute to cook out the raw taste.
Pour in the wine and stock, scraping up the browned bits, then return the chicken and bacon with the thyme and bay.
Cover and simmer gently for about 75 minutes until the chicken is fork-tender.
Saute the mushrooms in butter separately until golden and stir them into the braise.
Simmer uncovered to thicken the sauce, then discard the herbs and serve hot.
tip: Choose a wine you would happily drink; a harsh bottle makes a harsh sauce.

title: Engagement Roast Chicken
intro: A lemon-and-herb roast so reliably delicious it has a reputation for winning hearts. The bird perfumes with garlic and citrus while the pan juices become a bright, effortless sauce.
steps: Heat the oven to 425F and pat the chicken dry, seasoning the cavity well with salt and pepper.
Stuff the cavity with the thyme, both lemon halves, and the halved head of garlic.
Brush the skin all over with melted butter and season generously.
Scatter the sliced onion across a roasting pan and set the chicken on top.
Roast for about 70 minutes until the juices run clear and the skin is bronzed.
Rest the chicken loosely tented with foil for 15 minutes.
Deglaze the onions and pan drippings with stock over the stove, then strain into a sauce to spoon over the carved bird.
tip: Squeeze the roasted lemon from the cavity into the pan sauce for a bright lift.

title: Crispy Chicken with Garlic Butter
intro: Pan-seared chicken breasts finished with a foaming garlic-thyme butter that bastes the meat to a glossy shine. Fast, restaurant-sharp, and endlessly repeatable on a weeknight.
steps: Season the chicken generously and heat the oil in a heavy skillet until shimmering.
Lay the breasts skin-side down and press gently, cooking undisturbed until deeply golden.
Flip the chicken and add the butter, smashed garlic, and thyme to the pan.
Tilt the skillet and spoon the foaming butter continuously over the meat for two minutes.
Cook until the internal temperature reaches 165F, then squeeze in the lemon.
Rest the chicken for five minutes before slicing.
Serve drizzled with the garlic butter from the pan.
tip: Start the chicken in a not-too-hot pan so the skin renders slowly and crisps evenly.

title: Chicken Tikka Masala
intro: Yogurt-marinated chicken charred and simmered in a creamy, spiced tomato sauce that clings to every piece. Fragrant with garam masala and just enough chili to warm the back of the throat.
steps: Marinate the chicken in yogurt, half the ginger-garlic paste, and half the spices for at least an hour.
Sear the marinated chicken in a hot pan until charred at the edges, then set aside.
Soften the onion in ghee, then stir in the remaining ginger-garlic paste and spices until fragrant.
Pour in the crushed tomatoes and simmer until the sauce darkens and thickens.
Blend the sauce smooth if you like, then return it to the pan and stir in the cream.
Add the seared chicken and simmer gently until cooked through and coated.
Finish with a pinch of garam masala and serve with rice or naan.
tip: A quick blitz under the broiler on the marinated chicken mimics tandoor char at home.

title: Wiener Schnitzel-Style Chicken
intro: Thin chicken cutlets pounded delicate, breaded, and fried until impossibly crisp and golden. A squeeze of lemon is all this Austrian-inspired classic needs.
steps: Pound each butterflied breast between plastic wrap until thin and even.
Season the flour with salt, pepper, and paprika, then set up flour, egg, and breadcrumb stations.
Dredge each cutlet in flour, dip in egg, then coat fully in breadcrumbs.
Heat the oil in a wide skillet until a crumb sizzles on contact.
Fry the cutlets one or two at a time until golden brown on each side.
Drain on paper towels and season lightly with salt.
Serve at once with lemon wedges for squeezing.
tip: Swirl the pan as the cutlet fries so hot oil washes over the top and puffs the crust.

title: Chicken Cacciatore
intro: A rustic hunter's braise of chicken with peppers, tomatoes, and herbs that tastes like a slow Sunday in the Italian countryside. The sauce reduces into something rich enough to mop with crusty bread.
ingredients: 1 can (28 oz) whole tomatoes, crushed by hand
steps: Season the chicken and brown it well in olive oil, then remove to a plate.
Soften the onion, pepper, and mushrooms in the same pan until they release their liquid.
Add the garlic and cook until fragrant, then pour in the wine to deglaze.
Stir in the crushed tomatoes and rosemary and bring to a simmer.
Nestle the chicken back into the sauce and cover partially.
Braise gently for about 40 minutes until the chicken is tender and the sauce thickens.
Adjust seasoning and serve over polenta or with warm bread.
tip: Crushing whole tomatoes by hand gives the sauce a livelier texture than pureed ones.

title: Chicken Suprême with Pan Sauce
intro: A skin-on chicken breast cooked to a burnished crisp, then paired with a glossy, mustard-tinged pan sauce. Elegant French technique that fits a single skillet.
steps: Season the breasts and place skin-side down in a cold skillet with a little butter.
Cook over medium heat so the fat renders and the skin turns crisp and golden.
Flip and finish cooking until the meat reaches 160F, then rest on a plate.
Soften the shallot in the pan drippings, then pour in the wine and reduce by half.
Add the stock and simmer until slightly thickened.
Whisk in the mustard, cream, and remaining butter off the heat, then fold in the tarragon.
Slice the chicken and spoon the sauce generously over the top.
tip: Starting the skin in a cold pan renders the fat gently for a shatteringly crisp finish.

title: Berbere Spiced Chicken Stew
intro: An Ethiopian-rooted doro wat with chicken simmered in a deep, spice-layered onion sauce built on fiery berbere. Slow-cooked onions give it a jammy sweetness that balances the heat.
steps: Cook the onions in a dry pot over low heat, stirring often, until soft and deeply reduced.
Stir in the niter kibbeh and ginger-garlic paste and cook until fragrant.
Add the berbere and tomato paste and toast briefly to bloom the spices.
Pour in the stock and bring to a gentle simmer.
Nestle in the drumsticks, cover, and braise until the meat is falling-tender.
Score the hard-boiled eggs and add them to warm through in the sauce.
Season with salt and serve with injera or rice.
tip: Take your time softening the onions; that slow foundation defines the whole stew.

title: Za'atar Roast Chicken with Sumac
intro: Chicken thighs roasted with red onion, za'atar, and tangy sumac until the skin crisps and the pan fills with bright, herby juices. A vibrant sheet-pan dinner bursting with Middle Eastern flavor.
steps: Whisk the olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, za'atar, and half the sumac into a marinade.
Toss the chicken and onion wedges in the marinade and let sit for 30 minutes.
Arrange everything skin-side up on a sheet pan and season with salt and pepper.
Roast at 425F until the skin is crisp and the onions are charred at the edges.
Check that the thighs reach 175F for tender, pull-apart meat.
Scatter the remaining sumac and fresh parsley over the top.
Serve with the pan juices and warm flatbread.
tip: Sprinkle the second hit of sumac after roasting to keep its lemony tang bright.

title: Chicken in Milk
intro: A whole chicken pot-roasted in milk with lemon zest, garlic, and sage until the liquid breaks into golden, savory curds. Strange to read, unforgettable to eat.
ingredients: Zest of 2 lemons, peeled in strips
steps: Season the chicken and brown it all over in butter and oil in a snug Dutch oven.
Remove the chicken and pour off most of the excess fat.
Return the chicken to the pot and add the milk, cinnamon, lemon zest, garlic, and sage.
Bring to a bare simmer, then cover partially and transfer to a 375F oven.
Cook for about 90 minutes, basting once or twice, until the chicken is tender and the sauce curdles into golden nuggets.
Rest the chicken briefly, then break it into portions.
Spoon the curds, garlic, and lemony sauce over each serving.
tip: Do not worry when the milk splits; those savory curds are the whole point of the dish.

title: Spatchcock Harissa Chicken
intro: A flattened chicken slathered in smoky harissa and honey, roasted fast and hot so every inch turns lacquered and crisp. Bold, sticky, and made for tearing apart at the table.
steps: Whisk the harissa, honey, oil, lemon juice, garlic, and cumin into a thick glaze.
Pat the spatchcocked chicken dry and rub the glaze over every surface.
Let it marinate for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor.
Lay the chicken skin-side up and flat on a lined sheet pan.
Roast at 425F until the skin is deeply caramelized and lacquered.
Confirm the thickest part of the thigh reaches 175F.
Rest for 10 minutes, then cut into pieces and serve with the pan juices.
tip: Spatchcocking flattens the bird so it cooks evenly and much faster than a whole roast.

title: Buttermilk Fried Chicken
intro: Chicken brined and soaked in herby buttermilk, then fried in a craggy, well-seasoned crust that stays crackling long after it leaves the oil. This is the gold standard of fried chicken.
steps: Toss the chicken with two tablespoons of salt and refrigerate for an hour to brine.
Submerge the chicken in buttermilk and let it soak for at least 30 minutes.
Whisk the flour with the remaining salt, garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, and cayenne.
Lift each piece from the buttermilk and dredge heavily in the seasoned flour, pressing to build craggy crust.
Heat the oil to 320F and fry the dark meat first, then the white, adjusting heat to hold temperature.
Fry each piece until deep golden and cooked to 165F inside.
Drain on a rack and rest a few minutes before serving.
tip: Fry at a moderate temperature so the thick crust cooks through without burning.

title: Chicken Marbella
intro: A sweet-and-savory dinner-party icon of chicken baked with prunes, olives, capers, and brown sugar until glazed and jammy. It tastes even better made a day ahead.
steps: Combine the chicken with garlic, oregano, prunes, olives, capers, vinegar, and oil in a bowl.
Cover and marinate in the fridge overnight for the deepest flavor.
Arrange the chicken and marinade in a single layer in a baking dish.
Sprinkle the brown sugar over the chicken and pour the white wine around it.
Bake at 375F, basting occasionally, until the chicken is glazed and cooked through.
Let it rest a few minutes so the juices settle.
Serve with the prunes, olives, and pan sauce spooned over the top.
tip: Marinating overnight is what makes this dish sing, so plan a day ahead.

title: Butter Chicken
intro: Tender chicken bathed in a silky tomato sauce mellowed with butter, cream, and warm spices. Rich and gently sweet, it is comfort food that feels like a celebration.
steps: Marinate the chicken in yogurt, half the ginger-garlic paste, and the chili powder for an hour.
Sear the chicken in a hot pan until lightly charred, then set aside.
Melt the butter and cook the remaining ginger-garlic paste with the cumin and garam masala.
Pour in the tomato puree and simmer until it thickens and deepens in color.
Stir in the cream and crushed fenugreek leaves for a smooth, glossy sauce.
Return the chicken and simmer gently until cooked through and tender.
Finish with a knob of butter and serve with basmati rice or naan.
tip: Rubbing dried fenugreek leaves between your palms before adding releases their aroma.

title: Beef Wellington
intro: A center-cut fillet wrapped in mushroom duxelles, prosciutto and golden puff pastry. This showstopper carves into rosy slices with a crisp, buttery crust.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the fillet generously, then sear in hot oil on all sides until deeply browned and immediately brush all over with mustard.
Blitz the mushrooms and fry in a dry pan until the moisture evaporates into a dry paste, then cool completely.
Lay overlapping prosciutto on cling film, spread the duxelles over it, and set the fillet on top.
Roll the film tightly to form a firm cylinder and chill for 20 minutes to hold its shape.
Unwrap onto rolled puff pastry, seal the edges, brush with egg yolk and score lightly.
Bake at 200 C for about 35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden and the center reaches 52 C for medium-rare.
Rest for 10 minutes before slicing into thick portions.
tip: Chill the wrapped log twice so the pastry stays crisp and the meat cooks evenly.

title: Boeuf Bourguignon
intro: Tender beef braised slowly in red Burgundy with bacon, pearl onions and mushrooms. This rustic French classic rewards patience with a deep, silky sauce.
ingredients: 1.4 kg beef chuck, cut into large cubes
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Fry the lardons until crisp, remove, then brown the seasoned beef in the rendered fat in batches.
Soften the chopped onion, carrot and garlic, then stir in tomato paste and dust with flour.
Return the beef and bacon, pour in the wine and stock, and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cover and braise in a 160 C oven for about two and a half hours until fork-tender.
Meanwhile brown the pearl onions and mushrooms separately in butter until glossy.
Fold the onions and mushrooms into the stew and simmer for 15 more minutes.
Skim any fat, adjust the seasoning, and serve over mashed potatoes.
tip: Use a wine you would actually drink; a poor bottle leaves the sauce thin and sour.

title: Perfect Ribeye Steak
intro: A thick ribeye basted in foaming butter with thyme and garlic until it forms a mahogany crust. Restaurant-quality steak built on patience and constant basting.
steps: Pat the steaks dry, season heavily with salt, and let them sit at room temperature for 30 minutes.
Heat oil in a heavy skillet until it just begins to smoke.
Lay the steaks away from you and sear undisturbed for three minutes until a crust forms.
Flip, add butter, thyme and garlic, and tilt the pan to spoon the foaming butter over the meat.
Continue basting for three to four minutes until the center reaches 52 C for medium-rare.
Rest on a rack for five minutes, then finish with flaky salt and cracked pepper.
tip: A dry surface is the secret to browning, so never salt and sear a wet steak.

title: Chimichurri Skirt Steak
intro: Charred skirt steak sliced against the grain and drenched in bright, herby chimichurri. Smoky, garlicky and vivid with fresh parsley and red wine vinegar.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Stir together parsley, oregano, garlic, olive oil, vinegar and pepper flakes, then season and set aside.
Pat the steak dry, rub with neutral oil, and season aggressively with salt and pepper.
Heat a grill or cast-iron pan until very hot.
Sear the steak for about three minutes per side for medium-rare.
Rest the meat for five minutes to keep the juices in.
Slice thinly against the grain and spoon plenty of chimichurri over the top.
tip: Let the chimichurri sit at least 15 minutes so the garlic and herbs mellow together.

title: Classic Cheeseburger
intro: Juicy smash-style patties with crisp lacy edges and melted American cheese. Piled into a toasted bun with all the diner-style trimmings.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Divide the beef into four loose balls without overworking the meat.
Butter and toast the cut sides of the buns until golden.
Press each ball hard onto a screaming-hot griddle to form a thin patty with craggy edges.
Season, cook two minutes until the edges crisp, then flip and top with cheese.
Cook one more minute until the cheese melts, adding a splash of water under a lid to help it along.
Build each burger on a toasted bun with onion, pickles, lettuce and tomato.
tip: Do not press the patty after flipping or you will squeeze out the juices.

title: Beef Bolognese Ragu
intro: A slow-simmered ragu of beef, soffritto and tomato enriched with a splash of milk. Deeply savory and perfect clinging to fresh tagliatelle.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Fry the pancetta until its fat renders, then add the onion, carrot, celery and garlic and soften slowly.
Raise the heat, add the beef, and brown well while breaking up any clumps.
Stir in the tomato paste, then pour in the wine and let it reduce by half.
Add the chopped tomatoes, milk and stock, then bring to a bare simmer.
Cook uncovered on low heat for at least two hours, stirring now and then.
Season to taste once the sauce is thick and glossy.
Toss with cooked tagliatelle and a little pasta water to coat.
tip: The milk tenderizes the meat and rounds out the acidity, so do not skip it.

title: Steak au Poivre
intro: Peppercorn-crusted filet mignon in a silky cognac and cream pan sauce. A bistro classic that feels effortlessly elegant.
steps: Press the crushed peppercorns firmly into both sides of the salted steaks.
Sear in hot oil and butter for about three minutes per side for medium-rare, then rest on a plate.
Soften the shallot in the same pan, then remove from the heat and add the cognac.
Return to the heat carefully to flame off the alcohol, then pour in the stock.
Reduce by half, stir in the cream, and simmer until the sauce coats a spoon.
Return any resting juices to the sauce and spoon it generously over the steaks.
tip: Crack the peppercorns coarsely so they crackle in the crust rather than turning to dust.

title: Slow-Braised Short Ribs
intro: Meaty short ribs braised in red wine until they surrender off the bone. The glossy reduction turns into a luxurious, spoonable sauce.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the ribs well and sear in hot oil until browned on every side, then set aside.
Soften the onion, carrot, celery and garlic in the same pot until lightly colored.
Stir in the tomato paste, then deglaze with the wine and reduce by half.
Return the ribs, add the stock and rosemary, and bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise in a 150 C oven for about two and a half hours until tender.
Lift out the ribs and strain the sauce, then reduce it on the stove until glossy.
Spoon the sauce over the ribs to serve.
tip: Make it a day ahead and lift off the solidified fat for a cleaner, richer sauce.

title: Triple-Cooked Sunday Roast Beef
intro: A slow-roasted rib of beef cooked low for an even blush, then blasted for a burnished crust. Served with a deep, savory pan gravy.
steps: Salt the roast heavily and leave it uncovered in the fridge overnight to dry the surface.
Sear all sides in hot oil until browned, then set it on the onions and thyme in a roasting tray.
Roast at 120 C until the center reaches 50 C, roughly one and a half hours.
Rest the meat loosely tented while the oven heats to 240 C.
Return the roast for about ten minutes to crisp and darken the exterior.
Rest again, then make gravy by whisking flour into the pan juices with wine and stock.
Carve thick slices and pour the strained gravy over the top.
tip: Use a probe thermometer, as the low temperature makes visual doneness cues unreliable.

title: Korean Beef Bulgogi
intro: Thin ribeye marinated in soy, pear and sesame, then seared hot and fast. Sweet, savory and smoky, it is a Korean barbecue favorite.
steps: Whisk the pear, soy, sugar, sesame oil, garlic and ginger into a marinade.
Toss the sliced beef in the marinade and chill for at least 30 minutes.
Heat neutral oil in a wide pan or griddle until very hot.
Sear the beef in a single layer in batches so it caramelizes rather than steams.
Cook each batch two to three minutes until browned at the edges.
Scatter with spring onions and sesame seeds and serve with steamed rice.
tip: Freeze the ribeye for 30 minutes before slicing to get paper-thin pieces.

title: Texas-Style Beef Chili
intro: A no-beans chili of cubed chuck simmered in a rich dried-chile sauce. Bold, smoky and thickened by the beef itself as it breaks down.
ingredients: 1.3 kg beef chuck, cut into small cubes
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the beef and brown it in hot oil in batches, then set aside.
Soften the onion and garlic in the same pot until fragrant.
Stir in the chili powder, cumin and paprika and toast for one minute.
Return the beef, add the tomatoes and stock, and bring to a simmer.
Cover partially and cook on low for about two hours until the beef is tender.
Whisk the masa harina with a little water and stir it in to thicken the chili.
Simmer ten more minutes, then season to taste and serve.
tip: Toasting the spices before adding liquid deepens their flavor dramatically.

title: Beef Stroganoff
intro: Strips of tender beef and mushrooms in a tangy sour cream and mustard sauce. A quick, comforting classic served over buttered noodles.
ingredients: 600 g beef sirloin, cut into strips
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Season the beef strips, then sear quickly in hot oil until browned and remove.
Melt the butter and soften the onion and mushrooms until golden.
Stir in the mustard and paprika and cook for one minute.
Pour in the stock and simmer to reduce slightly.
Lower the heat and stir in the sour cream without letting it boil.
Return the beef with any juices and warm through for two minutes.
Serve immediately over buttered noodles or rice.
tip: Add the sour cream off a rolling boil so the sauce stays smooth and does not split.

title: Bo Ssam Slow-Roasted Pork Shoulder
intro: A whole pork shoulder cured in sugar and salt, then roasted until it shreds at the touch of a fork and finished with a burnished sweet crust. Wrap the smoky meat in crisp lettuce with rice, oysters, and two punchy sauces for a table everyone digs into together.
ingredients: 0.5 cup gochujang mixed with 2 tbsp sesame oil
steps: Rub the pork all over with the white sugar and 1 cup of the salt, then refrigerate uncovered for at least 6 hours or overnight.
Heat the oven to 300 F and set the pork in a roasting pan, discarding any juices that have pooled.
Roast for about 6 hours, basting with the pan drippings every hour, until the meat pulls apart easily.
Stir the brown sugar with the remaining tablespoon of salt and pat it over the top of the pork.
Raise the oven to 500 F and roast for 10 to 15 minutes until a dark, glossy crust forms.
Rest the pork for 15 minutes, then break it into chunks at the table.
Serve with lettuce leaves, warm rice, kimchi, gochujang sauce, and ginger scallion sauce for wrapping.
tip: The overnight sugar-salt cure is what gives the shoulder its deep seasoning, so do not skip the resting time in the fridge.

title: Beer-Braised Carnitas Tacos
intro: Cubes of pork shoulder simmer low in their own fat with orange, bay, and a splash of dark beer until meltingly tender. A final blast of high heat crisps the edges into the crackly, juicy carnitas that make a taco unforgettable.
ingredients: 3 lb boneless pork shoulder, cut in 2-inch cubes
1 cup chopped cilantro and diced onion, for serving
steps: Pile the pork, onion, garlic, bay leaves, salt, and lard into a heavy pot and squeeze in the orange juice before dropping in the spent halves.
Pour in the beer and enough water to nearly cover the meat, then bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook uncovered over low heat for about 2 hours, stirring occasionally, until the liquid has mostly evaporated.
Fish out and discard the orange, onion, garlic, and bay leaves.
Continue cooking in the rendered fat, undisturbed, until the pork browns and crisps on the bottom.
Turn the pieces once to crisp the second side, then shred coarsely with two forks.
Pile into warm tortillas and top with cilantro and onion.
tip: Resist stirring during the final browning stage so the meat develops those prized crunchy edges.

title: Perfect Slow-Roast Pork Belly with Crackling
intro: A slab of pork belly roasted over a bed of fennel and onion until the meat turns silky and the scored skin shatters into golden crackling. It is the kind of centerpiece that draws a gasp when it hits the table.
steps: Pat the skin bone dry, rub in the sea salt and crushed fennel seeds, and leave uncovered in the fridge for at least 2 hours.
Heat the oven to 425 F and scatter the fennel, onion, and garlic in a roasting tray with the olive oil.
Set the belly skin-side up on the vegetables and roast for 30 minutes until the crackling begins to blister.
Lower the oven to 325 F, pour the wine and stock into the tray around the meat, and roast for a further 2.5 to 3 hours.
Check that the meat is fork-tender and the crackling is crisp, returning it briefly to high heat if the skin needs more color.
Rest the belly on a board for 15 minutes while you strain the pan juices into a small pan.
Carve into thick slices and serve with the roasted vegetables and pan gravy.
tip: Bone-dry skin is non-negotiable for crackling, so pat it repeatedly and give it that uncovered fridge time.

title: Sticky Chinese Char Siu Pork
intro: Strips of pork shoulder marinated in honey, hoisin, and five-spice, then roasted until lacquered a deep mahogany. Sweet, savory, and glossy, it is the barbecue pork that hangs in every Cantonese window.
ingredients: 2 lb boneless pork shoulder, cut in long strips
2 tbsp honey, plus extra for glazing
0.5 tsp red fermented bean curd, optional
steps: Whisk the hoisin, honey, soy, wine, sugar, five-spice, garlic, and bean curd into a smooth marinade.
Coat the pork strips thoroughly and marinate in the fridge for at least 3 hours or overnight.
Heat the oven to 425 F and set a rack over a foil-lined tray filled with a little water.
Lay the pork on the rack and roast for 20 minutes.
Turn the strips, brush with reserved marinade, and roast for another 15 minutes.
Brush generously with warmed honey and blast under the broiler for 2 to 3 minutes until caramelized at the edges.
Rest for 5 minutes, then slice across the grain and serve with steamed rice.
tip: The tray of water beneath the rack keeps drips from burning and helps the pork stay juicy.

title: Cider-Braised Pork Chops with Apples
intro: Thick pork chops seared golden and finished in a pan of bubbling cider, soft onions, and wedges of sweet apple. It is honest autumn cooking that comes together in a single pan.
ingredients: 4 bone-in pork chops, about 1 inch thick
steps: Season the chops well and sear them in the butter and oil over medium-high heat until deeply browned on both sides.
Lift the chops out and soften the sliced onion in the same pan for a few minutes.
Add the apple wedges and cook until they take on a little color.
Pour in the cider, scrape up the browned bits, and stir in the mustard.
Return the chops to the pan, cover, and simmer gently for 12 to 15 minutes until cooked through.
Lift the chops and apples onto plates, then stir the cream into the pan and bubble to a light sauce.
Spoon the sauce over the chops and serve at once.
tip: Let the chops rest a couple of minutes off the heat so the juices settle before you spoon on the sauce.

title: Slow-Cooked Porchetta with Herb and Garlic
intro: Pork loin wrapped in its own belly, rolled around a fragrant paste of fennel, garlic, and rosemary, then roasted until the outside crackles and the inside stays succulent. This Italian showstopper carves into spirals of herb-flecked meat and shattering skin.
ingredients: 1 pork belly with attached loin, about 6 lb, skin scored
steps: Pound the fennel seeds, garlic, rosemary, sage, lemon zest, salt, and pepper into a coarse paste with the olive oil.
Lay the pork flat skin-side down and rub the herb paste over the meaty interior.
Roll the belly tightly around the loin and tie firmly with butcher's twine at regular intervals.
Rub the skin with a little oil and salt, then chill uncovered for at least 2 hours to dry.
Roast at 425 F for 30 minutes to blister the skin, then lower to 325 F for about 2.5 hours.
Confirm the center reaches 145 F and the crackling is crisp all over.
Rest for 20 minutes before removing the twine and carving into thick rounds.
tip: Ask your butcher for a belly-and-loin joint scored to the flesh, which makes rolling and crackling far easier.

title: Sticky Bourbon and Brown Sugar Baby Back Ribs
intro: Racks of baby back ribs rubbed with spice, baked low until tender, then mopped in a smoky bourbon glaze and caramelized under the broiler. Every rib pulls clean off the bone with a sweet, sticky finish.
ingredients: 2 racks baby back ribs, about 4 lb
steps: Peel the papery membrane from the back of each rack and pat the ribs dry.
Mix the brown sugar, paprika, garlic powder, salt, and pepper, then rub it all over the ribs.
Wrap each rack in foil, set on a tray, and bake at 300 F for about 2.5 hours until tender.
Simmer the ketchup, bourbon, and vinegar in a small pan for 10 minutes to make the glaze.
Unwrap the ribs, brush thickly with the glaze, and place them on a foil-lined tray.
Broil for 5 to 8 minutes, brushing once more, until the sauce bubbles and darkens.
Rest for 5 minutes, then cut between the bones to serve.
tip: Removing the membrane lets the rub and glaze penetrate and keeps the ribs from curling as they cook.

title: Vietnamese Caramel Braised Pork
intro: Chunks of pork belly braised in a homemade caramel with fish sauce and black pepper until glossy and deeply savory-sweet. Served over rice, this thit kho is comforting Vietnamese home cooking at its finest.
ingredients: 2 lb pork belly, cut in 1.5-inch pieces
steps: Melt the sugar in a heavy pot over medium heat, swirling until it turns a deep amber caramel.
Carefully add the shallots and garlic and stir for 30 seconds to bloom their aroma.
Add the pork belly and toss to coat every piece in the caramel.
Pour in the fish sauce, black pepper, and coconut water, then bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise gently for about 45 minutes until the pork is tender.
Slip in the peeled eggs and simmer uncovered for 15 minutes until the sauce thickens and glazes the meat.
Scatter with green onions and serve over steamed rice.
tip: Take the caramel to a rich amber but pull it before it smokes, as burnt caramel turns the whole braise bitter.

title: Pork Vindaloo
intro: A fiery Goan curry of pork simmered in a tangy paste of vinegar, dried chiles, and warm spices. Bright, hot, and layered, vindaloo rewards a little patience with extraordinary depth.
ingredients: 2.5 lb boneless pork shoulder, cut in 1.5-inch cubes
steps: Blend the soaked chiles, vinegar, cumin, peppercorns, garlic, and ginger into a smooth spice paste.
Coat the pork cubes in half the paste and marinate for at least 1 hour.
Heat the oil in a heavy pot and fry the onions until deep golden.
Stir in the remaining paste and the turmeric and cook until the oil separates from the mixture.
Add the marinated pork and sear, stirring, until the meat is coated and lightly browned.
Pour in enough water to nearly cover, bring to a simmer, then cover and cook for about 1 hour.
Uncover and simmer until the sauce thickens and clings to the tender pork.
tip: Kashmiri chiles give a vivid red color with moderate heat, so adjust the number up or down to suit your palate.

title: Tonkatsu Crispy Pork Cutlet
intro: Boneless pork loin pounded thin, coated in airy panko, and fried until shatteringly crisp and golden. Sliced and fanned over rice with a tangy sauce, it is Japanese comfort food done right.
ingredients: 4 boneless pork loin cutlets, about 5 oz each
steps: Pound the cutlets to an even half-inch thickness and season both sides with salt and white pepper.
Set up three bowls holding the flour, beaten eggs, and panko.
Dredge each cutlet in flour, dip in egg, then press firmly into the panko to coat.
Heat about half an inch of oil to 340 F in a wide skillet.
Fry the cutlets two at a time for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through.
Drain on a rack and rest for 2 minutes while you stir together the ketchup, Worcestershire, and oyster sauce.
Slice the cutlets crosswise into strips and serve with the sauce and steamed rice.
tip: A wire rack rather than paper towels keeps the underside of the tonkatsu crisp while it rests.

title: Pan-Seared Scallops with Cauliflower Puree
intro: Sweet sea scallops meet a silky cauliflower puree and a nutty brown-butter finish. This elegant plate balances restraint with deep, buttery flavor.
ingredients: 12 large dry sea scallops, side muscle removed
1 medium head cauliflower, cut into florets
Kosher salt and white pepper to taste
steps: Simmer the cauliflower in the milk with a pinch of salt until very tender, about 12 minutes.
Blend the cauliflower with 2 tablespoons butter and a splash of the cooking milk until glossy and smooth, then season and keep warm.
Pat the scallops completely dry and season both flat sides with salt and white pepper.
Heat the oil in a heavy skillet over high heat until shimmering, then lay the scallops down without crowding.
Sear undisturbed until a deep golden crust forms, about 2 minutes, then flip and cook 45 seconds more.
Lower the heat, add the remaining butter and let it foam and brown, then stir in the capers and lemon juice.
Spoon the puree onto warm plates, arrange the scallops, and finish with brown butter and chives.
tip: Use dry-packed scallops and a bone-dry surface; any moisture steams the pan and prevents that prized crust.

title: Nobu-Style Miso Black Cod
intro: Buttery black cod marinated in sweet-savory miso caramelizes into a glossy, umami-rich glaze. A modern Japanese classic that tastes far more complex than its effort.
ingredients: 4 black cod fillets, about 6 ounces each
steps: Simmer the sake and mirin in a small saucepan for 2 minutes to cook off the alcohol.
Whisk in the miso and sugar over low heat until fully dissolved, then cool the marinade completely.
Coat the cod fillets in the marinade, cover, and refrigerate for at least 24 hours.
Wipe excess marinade from the fillets and heat the oven broiler to high.
Sear the fillets briefly in an oiled ovenproof skillet until the tops begin to color.
Transfer to the broiler and cook until the glaze caramelizes and the fish flakes, about 5 minutes.
Plate the cod and garnish with pickled ginger and green onions.
tip: Marinate a full two to three days if you can; the miso gently cures the fish for a firmer, silkier texture.

title: Tuna Nicoise Salad
intro: Seared fresh tuna crowns a vibrant Provencal salad of green beans, potatoes, eggs, and olives. Bright, colorful, and made for a sunny table.
ingredients: Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Boil the potatoes until tender, adding the green beans for the final 3 minutes, then drain and cool.
Simmer the eggs for 7 minutes, cool in ice water, then peel and quarter.
Whisk the olive oil, vinegar, and mustard into a sharp dressing and season to taste.
Season the tuna steaks and sear in a hot pan for 1 to 2 minutes per side for a pink center.
Slice the tuna against the grain into thick strips.
Toss the potatoes, beans, tomatoes, and olives with half the dressing and arrange on a platter.
Top with the eggs and tuna, then drizzle with the remaining dressing.
tip: Keep the tuna rare in the middle; overcooking turns this luxurious fish dry and chalky.

title: Classic Moules Mariniere
intro: Plump mussels steamed open in white wine, shallots, and cream make a rustic seaside feast. Serve with crusty bread to soak up every drop of the fragrant broth.
ingredients: 4 pounds fresh mussels, scrubbed and debearded
Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Discard any mussels that are cracked or stay open when tapped.
Melt the butter in a large pot and soften the shallots and garlic without browning.
Pour in the wine with the bay leaf and bring to a lively simmer.
Add the mussels, cover tightly, and steam over high heat until the shells open, about 4 minutes.
Lift the mussels into bowls, discarding any that remain closed.
Stir the cream into the broth and warm through, then season to taste.
Pour the broth over the mussels and shower with parsley.
tip: Buy mussels the day you cook them and store loosely covered on ice; freshness is everything with shellfish.

title: Garlic Butter Shrimp Scampi
intro: Juicy shrimp swim in a glossy garlic, white wine, and lemon butter sauce tossed with linguine. Fast enough for a weeknight yet good enough for company.
ingredients: 1.25 pounds large shrimp, peeled and deveined
Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Cook the linguine in salted water until al dente, reserving a cup of pasta water before draining.
Season the shrimp and sear in the oil and 2 tablespoons butter until just pink, then set aside.
Lower the heat and soften the garlic and pepper flakes in the same pan for 30 seconds.
Pour in the wine and lemon juice, scraping up the browned bits, and simmer to reduce slightly.
Swirl in the remaining butter to build a silky sauce.
Return the shrimp and pasta to the pan, tossing with splashes of pasta water until coated.
Finish with parsley and a final squeeze of lemon.
tip: Do not overcook the shrimp; pull them the moment they curl into a loose C and turn opaque.

title: Baja Fish Tacos with Lime Crema
intro: Crisp beer-battered white fish tucked into warm tortillas with tangy slaw and lime crema. A sun-soaked coastal Mexican favorite bursting with texture.
ingredients: 1.5 pounds cod or halibut, cut into strips
steps: Whisk the flour, baking powder, and a pinch of salt with the cold beer into a smooth batter.
Stir the lime juice into the sour cream and season to make the crema.
Heat 2 inches of oil in a deep pan to 375 degrees Fahrenheit.
Dip the fish strips in the batter, letting excess drip off, and fry until golden and crisp.
Drain the fish on a rack and season immediately with salt.
Warm the tortillas until pliable and lightly toasted.
Fill each tortilla with fish, cabbage, cilantro, and a drizzle of lime crema.
tip: Keep the batter ice cold and the oil hot; the temperature contrast is what makes the crust shatteringly crisp.

title: Poached Halibut with Beurre Blanc
intro: Delicate halibut gently poached and cloaked in a tangy French butter sauce is seafood at its most refined. Every element whispers rather than shouts.
ingredients: Kosher salt and white pepper to taste
steps: Simmer the shallots, vinegar, and half the wine until the liquid nearly evaporates.
Whisk in the cold butter a few cubes at a time over low heat until the sauce is glossy and emulsified.
Strain the beurre blanc if desired, season with salt and white pepper, and keep barely warm.
Bring the fish stock and remaining wine to a gentle poach, never letting it boil.
Season the halibut and slip it into the liquid, poaching until just opaque, about 8 minutes.
Lift the fish out carefully and blot on a clean towel.
Nap each fillet with beurre blanc and scatter with chives.
tip: Keep the beurre blanc between warm and hot; too much heat breaks the emulsion into greasy pools.

title: Spanish Garlic Shrimp (Gambas al Ajillo)
intro: Sizzling shrimp bathed in olive oil, garlic, and a whisper of chili is the ultimate tapas showstopper. It comes to the table still bubbling and begging for bread.
ingredients: 1.25 pounds large shrimp, peeled and deveined
1 dried guindilla or arbol chili, crumbled
steps: Pat the shrimp dry and season lightly with salt.
Warm the olive oil with the garlic and chili in a wide skillet over medium heat until fragrant.
Once the garlic turns pale gold, raise the heat and add the shrimp in a single layer.
Cook the shrimp for about 1 minute per side until they just turn pink.
Stir in the smoked paprika and sherry and let it sizzle for 30 seconds.
Remove from the heat and shower with parsley.
Serve immediately with bread for mopping up the garlicky oil.
tip: Warm the garlic slowly so it infuses the oil without burning; scorched garlic turns the whole dish bitter.

title: Beer-Battered Fish and Chips
intro: Golden, crackling batter gives way to steamy flakes of white fish, served with thick chips and mushy peas. This is British comfort food done properly.
ingredients: 1.5 cups all-purpose flour, plus extra for dusting
2 pounds russet potatoes, cut into thick chips
Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Parboil the chips for 5 minutes, drain, and dry thoroughly on a towel.
Fry the chips at 300 degrees Fahrenheit until soft but pale, then set aside to cool.
Whisk the flour, baking powder, sparkling water, and beer into a smooth, cold batter.
Raise the oil to 375 degrees Fahrenheit, dust the fish in flour, then dip into the batter.
Fry the fish until deep golden and crisp, about 6 minutes, then drain on a rack.
Return the chips to the hot oil and fry until crisp and golden.
Warm the peas with butter and crush lightly, then serve alongside the fish and chips.
tip: Double-fry the chips and keep the batter fridge-cold; both tricks guarantee maximum crunch.

title: Roasted Salmon with Herb Salsa Verde
intro: Tender roasted salmon meets a punchy green herb sauce loaded with capers, garlic, and lemon. Fresh, bold, and effortlessly vibrant on the plate.
ingredients: 0.5 cup extra-virgin olive oil, plus more for the fish
Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Heat the oven to 425 degrees Fahrenheit and line a baking tray.
Rub the salmon with a little oil, season well, and place skin-side down on the tray.
Roast the salmon until just cooked through and barely translucent at the center, about 12 minutes.
Finely chop the parsley, mint, and capers together on a board.
Stir the herbs with the garlic, olive oil, lemon juice, and zest, then season the salsa verde.
Rest the salmon for a couple of minutes off the heat.
Spoon the salsa verde generously over each fillet before serving.
tip: Make the salsa verde fresh and use it within a few hours; the herbs dull and darken as it sits.

title: Maryland-Style Crab Cakes
intro: Sweet lump crab bound with just enough seasoned crumb fries into golden, crab-forward cakes. Minimal filler lets the shellfish shine.
ingredients: Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Whisk the egg, mayonnaise, mustard, Old Bay, parsley, and lemon juice into a smooth binder.
Fold the crabmeat and panko into the binder gently to keep the lumps intact.
Shape the mixture into four thick patties and chill for 30 minutes to firm up.
Melt the butter in a skillet over medium heat.
Cook the cakes until deeply golden on the first side, about 4 minutes.
Flip carefully and cook until the second side is crisp and the centers are hot.
Serve with lemon wedges and season to taste.
tip: Chill the shaped cakes before cooking so they hold together and develop a proper crust in the pan.

title: Whole Roasted Sea Bass with Fennel
intro: A whole sea bass roasted over fennel, lemon, and herbs delivers moist, aromatic flesh straight from the bone. Simple ingredients let the fish speak for itself.
ingredients: 2 whole sea bass, about 1.25 pounds each, cleaned and scaled
2 lemons, one sliced and one juiced
Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Heat the oven to 400 degrees Fahrenheit and toss the sliced fennel with half the olive oil.
Spread the fennel in a roasting dish and roast for 10 minutes to soften.
Score the fish skin, season inside and out, and stuff the cavities with lemon slices and herbs.
Lay the fish over the fennel, drizzle with the remaining oil, and pour the wine around.
Roast until the flesh is opaque and pulls easily from the bone, about 20 minutes.
Squeeze the lemon juice over the fish once out of the oven.
Fillet at the table and serve with the roasted fennel and pan juices.
tip: Check for doneness at the thickest part behind the head; the flesh should flake cleanly to the bone.

title: Salt and Pepper Squid
intro: Crisp, peppery squid with a light cornstarch coat delivers a shattering crunch and tender bite. A quick fry and a squeeze of lime make it irresistible.
ingredients: 1.5 pounds cleaned squid, bodies scored and cut into rings
steps: Pat the squid thoroughly dry to help the coating stick and prevent splatter.
Combine the cornstarch, flour, black pepper, salt, and five-spice in a bowl.
Toss the squid in the seasoned coating until evenly dusted, shaking off excess.
Heat 2 inches of oil to 375 degrees Fahrenheit in a deep pan.
Fry the squid in small batches for about 90 seconds until pale gold and crisp.
Drain on a rack and immediately toss with sliced chilies and green onions.
Serve hot with lime wedges for squeezing.
tip: Fry in small batches and keep the oil hot; crowding the pan drops the temperature and makes the squid rubbery.

title: Lobster Roll with Lemon Butter
intro: Sweet lobster meat warmed in lemon butter and piled into toasted buttery buns is New England luxury made simple. Every bite is rich, tender, and briny.
ingredients: 2 cooked lobsters, about 1.5 pounds each, meat removed and chopped
Kosher salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Melt 4 tablespoons butter with the lemon juice in a skillet over low heat.
Warm the lobster meat gently in the lemon butter just until heated through, then remove from heat.
Fold in the celery and chives and season with salt, pepper, and paprika.
Melt the remaining butter in a clean skillet.
Toast the buns on both flat sides until golden and crisp.
Pile the warm lobster into the toasted buns.
Finish with an extra sprinkle of chives and serve at once.
tip: Warm the lobster gently and briefly; high heat toughens the delicate meat and dries it out.

title: Cioppino Seafood Stew
intro: A San Francisco-born tomato stew brimming with mussels, clams, shrimp, and fish in a wine-laced broth. Rustic, generous, and made for sharing.
ingredients: 0.75 pound firm white fish, cut into chunks
0.75 pound large shrimp, peeled and deveined
steps: Soften the onion, fennel, and garlic in the olive oil with the pepper flakes until fragrant.
Pour in the wine and simmer until reduced by half.
Add the crushed tomatoes and fish stock, then simmer the broth for 15 minutes to deepen.
Add the clams and mussels, cover, and steam until the shells begin to open.
Slip in the fish chunks and shrimp and simmer gently until just cooked, about 5 minutes.
Discard any shellfish that stay closed and season the broth to taste.
Ladle into bowls and shower with parsley.
tip: Add the seafood in order of cooking time so nothing overcooks; the fish and shrimp go in last.

title: Classic French Onion Soup
intro: Deeply caramelized onions melt into a rich beef broth, crowned with a raft of toasted bread and bubbling Gruyere. This is the ultimate rainy-day comfort in a bowl.
steps: Melt the butter with the olive oil in a heavy pot over medium-low heat, then add the onions and cook covered for 15 minutes until softened.
Uncover, stir in the sugar and salt, and continue cooking for 40 minutes, stirring often, until the onions turn a deep golden brown.
Sprinkle in the flour and stir for 2 minutes, then pour in the wine and scrape up any browned bits from the bottom.
Add the beef broth and brandy, bring to a simmer, and cook gently for 30 minutes; season with pepper.
Toast the baguette slices under a broiler until crisp and dry on both sides.
Ladle the soup into ovenproof bowls, float a toast on each, and pile on the Gruyere.
Broil until the cheese is melted, bubbling, and browned at the edges, then serve immediately.
tip: Patience is everything with the onions; rushing the caramelization gives you a pale, thin soup instead of a deep, sweet one.

title: Roasted Tomato Basil Soup
intro: Oven-roasting the tomatoes concentrates their sweetness into a velvety, garden-fresh soup. A handful of basil at the end keeps it bright and lively.
steps: Toss the plum tomatoes with 1/4 cup olive oil, salt, and pepper, then roast at 400 degrees for 45 minutes.
Warm the remaining oil in a large pot and cook the onion and garlic over medium heat for 8 minutes until soft.
Add the canned tomatoes, basil, thyme, sugar, and chicken stock to the pot and stir to combine.
Scrape in the roasted tomatoes along with all their pan juices.
Bring to a boil, then lower the heat and simmer uncovered for 40 minutes.
Puree the soup with an immersion blender until smooth but still slightly textured.
Taste, adjust the salt, and serve hot with a drizzle of olive oil.
tip: Save a few extra basil leaves to slice thinly and scatter over each bowl for a fresh, fragrant finish.

title: Vietnamese Beef Pho
intro: A fragrant, deeply spiced broth built on charred aromatics and slow-simmered bones. Slurping this at a plastic stool on a Hanoi street corner is a rite of passage worth recreating at home.
ingredients: 4 pounds beef bones, preferably marrow and knuckle
4 inch piece of fresh ginger, halved lengthwise
1 tablespoon rock sugar or brown sugar
1/2 pound beef sirloin, sliced paper thin
1 bunch fresh Thai basil and cilantro
steps: Blanch the bones and brisket in boiling water for 5 minutes, then drain and rinse to remove impurities.
Char the onion and ginger directly over a flame or under a broiler until blackened and fragrant.
Toast the star anise, cinnamon, and cloves in a dry pan until aromatic, about 2 minutes.
Combine the bones, brisket, charred aromatics, and toasted spices in a large stockpot with 5 quarts of water and simmer gently for 3 hours, skimming often.
Remove the brisket after 90 minutes, slice it thinly, and set aside; season the broth with fish sauce, sugar, and salt.
Soak and cook the rice noodles according to the package, then divide them among deep bowls.
Top the noodles with sliced brisket and raw sirloin, ladle the boiling broth over to cook the raw beef, and serve with sprouts, herbs, and lime.
tip: Keep the broth at a bare simmer rather than a rolling boil, which keeps it clear and clean instead of cloudy.

title: Spiced Butternut Squash and Coconut Soup
intro: Silky roasted squash meets creamy coconut and a warm hum of chili and lime. It is bold, layered, and irresistibly comforting.
ingredients: 1 large butternut squash, peeled and cubed
steps: Toss the squash with 2 tablespoons of oil, the cumin, and coriander, then roast at 425 degrees for 30 minutes until tender and caramelized.
Heat the remaining oil in a pot and soften the onion, garlic, chili, and ginger over medium heat for 6 minutes.
Add the roasted squash, coconut milk, and stock, then bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook for 10 minutes to let the flavors meld together.
Blend the soup until completely smooth, adding a splash of water if it is too thick.
Stir in the salt and lime juice, tasting to balance the heat and acidity.
Serve topped with toasted pumpkin seeds and an extra pinch of chili.
tip: Roasting the squash rather than boiling it deepens the flavor and gives the soup a natural sweetness.

title: Ethiopian Berbere Lentil Stew
intro: Earthy red lentils simmer with fiery berbere spice and spiced butter into a thick, soulful stew. It is warming, fragrant, and deeply satisfying spooned over rice or with injera.
ingredients: 3 tablespoons niter kibbeh or unsalted butter
steps: Melt the butter in a large pot and cook the onions over medium heat for 10 minutes until deeply softened.
Stir in the garlic and ginger and cook for another 2 minutes until fragrant.
Add the berbere and tomato paste, stirring constantly for 2 minutes to toast the spices.
Pour in the lentils and stock, then bring to a boil.
Lower the heat and simmer uncovered for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lentils break down and thicken.
Season with salt and cardamom, adding water if the stew becomes too thick.
Finish with fresh cilantro and serve hot with rice or flatbread.
tip: Toast the berbere in the fat before adding liquid; it wakes up the spices and removes any raw, dusty edge.

title: Rustic Italian Ribollita
intro: A hearty Tuscan bread soup packed with cannellini beans, cavolo nero, and vegetables. Thick enough to stand a spoon in, it only gets better the next day.
ingredients: 1/4 cup olive oil, plus more for serving
1 can (28 ounces) whole tomatoes, crushed by hand
2 cans (14 ounces each) cannellini beans, drained
1 bunch cavolo nero, stems removed and leaves chopped
4 thick slices of stale sourdough bread
steps: Warm the olive oil in a large pot and cook the onion, carrots, and celery over medium heat for 12 minutes until soft.
Add the garlic and rosemary and cook for 2 minutes until aromatic.
Stir in the tomatoes and half of the beans, then simmer for 10 minutes.
Mash the remaining beans into a rough paste and add them to the pot along with the stock.
Add the cavolo nero, salt, and pepper, then simmer gently for 30 minutes.
Tear the bread into chunks, stir it into the soup, and cook for another 15 minutes until it melts into the broth.
Ladle into bowls and finish each with a generous drizzle of good olive oil.
tip: Stale bread works far better than fresh here, soaking up the broth without turning to mush.

title: Mexican Tortilla Soup
intro: A smoky, chile-scented broth crowned with crisp tortilla strips, creamy avocado, and tangy cheese. Every spoonful layers heat, richness, and crunch.
ingredients: 6 corn tortillas, cut into thin strips
2 dried pasilla chiles, stemmed and seeded
steps: Fry the tortilla strips in the oil until golden and crisp, then drain them on paper towels.
Toast the pasilla chiles in the same pan for 30 seconds per side, then tear one into small pieces.
Blend the tomatoes, onion, garlic, and the whole toasted chile into a smooth puree.
Pour the puree into a hot pot and cook, stirring, for 8 minutes until it darkens and thickens.
Add the chicken stock and salt, then simmer for 20 minutes.
Stir in the shredded chicken and warm through for 5 minutes.
Serve topped with tortilla strips, avocado, queso fresco, cilantro, the reserved chile pieces, and lime wedges.
tip: Frying your own tortilla strips gives far more flavor and crunch than store-bought chips.

title: Chicken and Wild Rice Soup
intro: A refined, creamy soup where nutty wild rice and tender chicken meet a delicate herb-scented broth. Elegant enough for company yet comforting enough for a quiet night in.
steps: Simmer the wild rice in 3 cups of water for 40 minutes until tender, then drain any excess liquid.
Melt the butter in a large pot and cook the onion, carrots, and celery over medium heat for 8 minutes.
Add the mushrooms and thyme and cook for another 6 minutes until they release their moisture.
Sprinkle in the flour and stir for 2 minutes to cook out the raw taste.
Gradually whisk in the chicken stock and bring to a gentle simmer for 10 minutes.
Stir in the cooked rice, shredded chicken, and cream, then season with salt and pepper.
Warm through for 5 minutes without boiling and serve hot.
tip: Cook the wild rice separately so it stays perfectly tender without clouding or thickening the broth too early.

title: Indian Mulligatawny Soup
intro: A golden, curry-spiced soup thickened with lentils and softened with coconut and apple. It balances warmth, sweetness, and gentle heat in every spoonful.
steps: Heat the ghee in a large pot and cook the onion over medium heat for 8 minutes until golden.
Add the garlic and ginger and cook for 2 minutes until fragrant.
Stir in the curry powder and turmeric and toast for 1 minute.
Add the lentils, grated apple, and stock, then bring to a boil.
Lower the heat and simmer for 30 minutes until the lentils are completely soft.
Blend the soup until smooth, then stir in the coconut milk and salt.
Finish with lemon juice and cilantro and serve over a spoonful of rice if you like.
tip: Toasting the spices in hot fat before adding liquid is the key to a rounded, fragrant flavor rather than a raw, powdery one.

title: Provencal Fish Soup with Rouille
intro: A sun-soaked soup of the French coast, fragrant with saffron, fennel, and orange. Served with a garlicky rouille-topped crouton, it brings the Mediterranean straight to your table.
ingredients: 2 pounds mixed firm white fish, cut into chunks
1/2 cup olive oil for the rouille
steps: Warm the olive oil in a large pot and soften the leek and fennel over medium heat for 10 minutes.
Add the minced garlic and cook for 2 minutes, then stir in the tomatoes.
Pour in the fish stock, saffron, and orange zest, then simmer for 20 minutes.
Add the fish chunks and poach gently for 10 minutes until just cooked through.
Whisk the egg yolk with the grated garlic, then slowly stream in the olive oil to make a thick rouille.
Season the soup with salt and pepper and remove the orange zest.
Ladle into bowls and serve with toasted baguette slices spread with rouille.
tip: Add the fish only at the very end and keep the heat gentle, since a hard boil will toughen and shred it.

title: Hungarian Goulash
intro: A rich, paprika-stained stew of tender beef, sweet peppers, and soft potatoes. Slow cooking coaxes out a depth of flavor that warms you from the inside out.
ingredients: 3 pounds beef chuck, cut into 1 inch cubes
steps: Heat the lard in a heavy pot and brown the beef in batches, then set the meat aside.
Lower the heat and cook the onions in the same pot for 12 minutes until soft and golden.
Remove the pot from the heat and stir in the paprika and caraway so they do not scorch.
Return the pot to the heat, add the garlic and tomato paste, and cook for 2 minutes.
Return the beef to the pot with the stock, then cover and simmer gently for 90 minutes.
Add the bell peppers and potatoes and cook for another 40 minutes until everything is tender.
Season with salt and pepper and serve with crusty bread or buttered noodles.
tip: Pull the pot off the heat before adding the paprika; burnt paprika turns bitter and can ruin the whole stew.

title: Moroccan Harira
intro: A fragrant tomato-based soup thick with chickpeas, lentils, and warm spices, brightened with lemon and herbs. It is the soul-warming bowl traditionally served to break the fast.
ingredients: 1/2 cup broken vermicelli or small pasta
steps: Warm the olive oil in a large pot and cook the onion and celery over medium heat for 8 minutes.
Add the garlic, ginger, cinnamon, and turmeric and stir for 1 minute until aromatic.
Stir in the crushed tomatoes and cook for 5 minutes to deepen the flavor.
Add the chickpeas, lentils, and stock, then bring to a boil.
Lower the heat and simmer for 45 minutes until the lentils are tender.
Add the vermicelli and cook for another 10 minutes until soft.
Season with salt, stir in the lemon juice and fresh herbs, and serve hot.
tip: Add the lemon juice off the heat right before serving to keep its brightness from cooking away.

title: Roasted Butternut Squash Salad with Tahini and Za'atar
intro: Sweet caramelized squash meets a nutty tahini drizzle and a bright scatter of herbs. This warm salad is generous, layered, and unmistakably Middle Eastern in spirit.
ingredients: 1 large butternut squash, peeled and cut into 2 cm wedges
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Heat the oven to 220C and toss the squash and onion with 2 tablespoons olive oil, salt, and pepper on a large tray.
Roast for 30 to 35 minutes until the edges are deeply caramelized and the flesh is tender.
Whisk together the tahini, garlic, lemon juice, water, and a pinch of salt until it forms a smooth pourable sauce.
Loosen the sauce with a little more water if needed so it drizzles easily.
Arrange the warm roasted vegetables across a serving platter in a loose single layer.
Spoon the tahini sauce generously over the top and scatter with toasted pine nuts.
Finish with a shower of za'atar and fresh cilantro leaves before serving warm.
tip: Roast the squash until the edges are almost burnt; that deep color is where the sweetness lives.

title: Barefoot Green Salad with Champagne Vinaigrette
intro: A crisp bowl of tender greens dressed in a bright, mustardy champagne vinaigrette. Simple, elegant, and the kind of salad that quietly steals the meal.
ingredients: 8 cups mixed baby greens and butter lettuce
Salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste
steps: Whisk the champagne vinegar, Dijon mustard, minced shallot, honey, salt, and pepper together in a small bowl.
Slowly stream in the olive oil while whisking until the dressing emulsifies and thickens slightly.
Wash and thoroughly dry the greens so the dressing clings rather than slides.
Place the greens in a large wide bowl just before serving.
Drizzle over just enough vinaigrette to lightly coat and toss gently with your hands.
Shave Parmesan over the top and add a final grind of black pepper before serving.
tip: Dress the greens only moments before serving so they stay lively and never wilt.

title: Jamie's Crunchy Rainbow Slaw with Lemon and Mint
intro: A riot of raw color tossed in a zippy lemon dressing with a lift of fresh mint. This is a fast, no-cook side that brings real crunch to any plate.
ingredients: 1 crisp apple, cut into thin matchsticks
1 small handful fresh mint leaves, chopped
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Combine the shredded cabbage, grated carrot, grated beetroot, and apple matchsticks in a large bowl.
Whisk the olive oil, lemon juice, honey, salt, and pepper together in a small jug.
Pour the dressing over the vegetables and toss well until everything glistens.
Let the slaw sit for five minutes so the flavors mingle and the cabbage softens slightly.
Fold through most of the chopped mint just before serving.
Scatter the remaining mint and toasted sunflower seeds over the top for crunch.
tip: Grate the beetroot last and toss quickly to keep its color from bleeding across the whole bowl.

title: Nigella's Warm Halloumi and Watermelon Salad
intro: Salty pan-fried halloumi against cool sweet watermelon is a study in glorious contrast. A handful of mint and a squeeze of lime pull it all together.
ingredients: 250 g halloumi, sliced into 1 cm slabs
600 g watermelon, cut into bite-sized cubes
2 tablespoons black olives, pitted and halved
steps: Pat the halloumi slices dry with paper towels so they sear rather than steam.
Heat the olive oil in a nonstick pan over medium-high heat until shimmering.
Fry the halloumi for two to three minutes per side until deeply golden and crisp.
Arrange the watermelon cubes across a serving platter and scatter over the olives.
Lay the hot halloumi over the watermelon while it is still sizzling.
Squeeze the lime juice over everything and grind on plenty of black pepper.
Finish with the sliced chili and torn mint leaves and serve immediately.
tip: Serve the moment the halloumi comes off the heat; it turns rubbery once it cools.

title: Chez Panisse Garden Lettuces with Baked Goat Cheese
intro: Warm, herb-crusted rounds of goat cheese rest on a bed of the tenderest garden lettuces. It is a quiet celebration of simple ingredients treated with real care.
ingredients: 150 g fresh goat cheese log, chilled
2 tablespoons olive oil for the cheese
Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Slice the chilled goat cheese into four rounds and brush each with olive oil.
Mix the breadcrumbs with the thyme, then press the rounds firmly into the crumbs to coat.
Chill the coated rounds for ten minutes while you heat the oven to 200C.
Bake the cheese rounds on a lined tray for about eight minutes until golden and just softened.
Whisk the red wine vinegar, Dijon, salt, and pepper, then stream in the dressing olive oil.
Toss the lettuces lightly with just enough vinaigrette to coat each leaf.
Divide the greens among plates and set a warm goat cheese round on each before serving.
tip: Keep the cheese cold right up until baking so it holds its shape and does not ooze away.

title: Giada's Italian Chopped Salad with Salami and Provolone
intro: Every forkful of this hearty chopped salad delivers salami, cheese, and crisp greens in one bite. A punchy oregano vinaigrette makes it taste like a great Italian deli.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Whisk the red wine vinegar, dried oregano, salt, and pepper in a large salad bowl.
Stream in the olive oil while whisking until the vinaigrette comes together.
Add the chopped romaine and radicchio directly on top of the dressing.
Pile in the diced salami, provolone, tomatoes, chickpeas, and pepperoncini.
Toss everything thoroughly so every piece is evenly coated and mingled.
Taste and adjust the salt, then serve right away while the greens are crisp.
tip: Dice all the add-ins to roughly the same size so each forkful is perfectly balanced.

title: Gordon's Seared Beef and Rocket Salad with Parmesan
intro: Rare seared steak fanned over peppery rocket makes a restaurant-worthy main-course salad. Sharp Parmesan and a mustard dressing give it real backbone.
ingredients: 400 g sirloin steak, at room temperature
Salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste
steps: Season the steak generously all over with salt and freshly ground pepper.
Heat the searing oil in a heavy pan until almost smoking, then sear the steak for two to three minutes per side for medium-rare.
Transfer the steak to a board and let it rest for at least five minutes.
Whisk the balsamic vinegar, Dijon, and extra virgin olive oil into a glossy dressing.
Toss the rocket and cherry tomatoes with just enough dressing to coat lightly.
Slice the rested steak thinly against the grain into elegant strips.
Fan the beef over the dressed leaves and finish with shaved Parmesan and a grind of pepper.
tip: Rest the steak fully before slicing so the juices stay in the meat instead of on the board.

title: Jose's Spanish Tomato Salad with Sherry Vinegar and Almonds
intro: Ripe summer tomatoes shine with nothing more than great olive oil, sherry vinegar, and crunchy Marcona almonds. This is Spanish simplicity at its most confident.
ingredients: 700 g mixed ripe tomatoes, cut into thick wedges
4 tablespoons Spanish extra virgin olive oil
1 small garlic clove, very finely grated
steps: Arrange the tomato wedges in a single layer across a wide serving plate.
Season them generously with flaky sea salt and let them sit for five minutes to draw out their juices.
Whisk the sherry vinegar, grated garlic, and olive oil into a bright dressing.
Spoon the dressing evenly over the salted tomatoes.
Scatter the chopped Marcona almonds across the top for crunch.
Finish with torn basil leaves and a final crack of black pepper before serving.
tip: Use tomatoes at full room temperature; refrigeration mutes their flavor and aroma.

title: Nicoise Salad with Seared Tuna and Green Beans
intro: This composed Provencal classic layers seared tuna, waxy potatoes, and crisp beans over bright leaves. Arranged with care, it is a complete and beautiful main-course salad.
ingredients: Salt and black pepper to taste
steps: Boil the potatoes until tender, then drain and halve them while still warm.
Cook the green beans in salted boiling water for three minutes, then plunge into ice water to keep them crisp.
Boil the eggs for eight minutes, cool them, then peel and quarter.
